title: Descend Into a Volcano: Iceland's Thríhnúkagígur Offers a Journey to an Empty Magma Chamber
summary:
Imagine standing inside a volcano, surrounded by walls that once held molten rock at temperatures over 2,000°F. That's exactly what visitors to Thríhnúkagígur (pronounced three-NOO-ka-gee-gur) in Iceland get to experience. This dormant volcano, whose name means 'Three Peaks Crater,' is the only place on Earth where humans can enter an empty magma chamber.

Located near Reykjavík, Thríhnúkagígur last erupted about 4,500 years ago. Unlike most volcanoes, where leftover magma slowly solidifies inside the chamber, here the magma vanished. Scientists are puzzled. 'It's like somebody came and pulled the plug and all the magma ran down out of it,' said Haraldur Sigurdsson, a volcanologist at the University of Rhode Island. The magma may have been sucked back down into the Earth's crust.

The chamber is about 700 feet (210 meters) deep — more than double the height of the Statue of Liberty including its pedestal. Visitors descend in an open cable elevator, originally built for scientists in 2010, and then explore a 33,600-square-foot (3,120 square meters) cave floor. The walls are a stunning palette: bronze, indigo, blue, yellow, and orange. These colors come from microbes (tiny organisms) and sulfur-rich gases that painted the rock over centuries. Occasionally, puffs of steam rise from corners, but these are just water droplets hitting hot lamps — not signs of an impending eruption.

Thrínúkagígur has erupted three times in the past 50,000 years. Its three peaks form a line, each about 115 feet tall and 660 feet apart. The youngest peak, from the most recent eruption, holds the entrance. The oldest peak, 50,000 years old, is made of hyaloclastite — a sharp, glassy rock formed when lava met ice during Iceland's ice age. Tours have been running since 2012, offering a rare glimpse into the plumbing of a volcano.
why_it_matters: Exploring Thríhnúkagígur helps scientists understand volcanic plumbing and how magma behaves, which could improve prediction of eruptions — knowledge that protects communities in volcanic regions worldwide.

keywords.explanation: A dormant volcano in Iceland, the only place where humans can enter an empty magma chamber
An underground cavity that once held molten rock; here it is empty and accessible
A volcanic island nation with many active and dormant volcanoes
An open lift used to descend 700 feet into the former magma chamber
Tiny organisms that produce pigments, coloring the chamber walls
A scientist who studies volcanoes, such as Haraldur Sigurdsson

questions.question: What makes Thríhnúkagígur unique among volcanoes?
options: It is the most active volcano in Iceland
It has an empty magma chamber that people can enter
It is the tallest volcano in the world
It has a lake of lava
questions.correct_answer: It has an empty magma chamber that people can enter

questions.question: How deep is the magma chamber?
What caused the colors on the chamber walls?
What is the leading hypothesis for the missing magma?
options: It drained back into the Earth's crust
questions.correct_answer: It drained back into the Earth's crust

questions.question: When did the most recent eruption of Thríhnúkagígur occur?
What is the oldest peak made of, and why?
options: Hyaloclastite, formed when lava met ice
questions.correct_answer: Hyaloclastite, formed when lava met ice

background_read: Iceland sits atop the Mid-Atlantic Ridge, where tectonic plates diverge, making it one of the most volcanically active places on Earth.
The term 'magma chamber' refers to a large pool of molten rock beneath a volcano; after an eruption, it usually solidifies into rock, but Thríhnúkagígur is an exception.
Hyaloclastite is a rock formed when lava erupts under ice, common in Iceland due to glaciers, and is very sharp.
Tours into the volcano have been operating since 2012, offering a rare glimpse into the 'plumbing' of a volcano.
Article_Structure: LEAD: 'Imagine standing inside a volcano, surrounded by walls that once held molten rock.'
└─ specific: Thríhnúkagígur is the only place where humans can enter an empty magma chamber.
KEY EVENT: Last eruption 4,500 years ago; magma disappeared mysteriously.
├─ HOW: 'Like somebody came and pulled the plug'
└─ SCIENTIFIC PUZZLE: Magma may have drained back into the Earth's crust.
├─ DEPTH: 700 feet (210 m) descent via open cable elevator.
├─ SIZE: Cave floor 33,600 sq ft (half a football field).
└─ COLORS: Bronze, blue, yellow, etc. from microbes and sulfur gases.
├─ THREE PEAKS: Formed over 50,000 years, youngest peak holds entrance.
├─ OLDEST PEAK: 50,000 years old, made of hyaloclastite.
OPEN QUESTION: Why did the magma drain away completely?

perspectives.description: This unique site offers an unparalleled educational and adventure experience, drawing tourists to Iceland and providing scientists with direct access to a magma chamber, advancing volcanic research.

Increasing tourism could damage the fragile rock formations and microbial ecosystems inside the chamber. There are concerns about over-visitation and the carbon footprint of flights and tours to Iceland.
The chamber's empty state remains a geological mystery. Studying it could reveal new insights into how magma moves underground, but the uniqueness makes it hard to generalize to other volcanoes.

If scientists can determine why the magma drained, it could lead to better models for predicting volcanic eruptions. Continued monitoring and research might also help identify other volcanoes with similar accessible chambers.
